metadata.description: Comprehensive accessibility standards catalog covering WCAG 2.1/2.2 Level A and AA, key AAA criteria, and ARIA patterns

standards.name: Non-text Content
standards.description: All non-text content presented to the user has a text alternative that serves the equivalent purpose, except for controls/input, time-based media, tests, sensory experiences, CAPTCHA, and decoration.
standards.intent: Ensures that all users, including those using screen readers or braille displays, can access information conveyed by non-text content such as images, icons, charts, and multimedia.
techniques: Provide alt text for informative images using the alt attribute
Use aria-label or aria-labelledby for icon buttons and controls
Provide long descriptions for complex images like charts and diagrams
Use empty alt attribute (alt="") for decorative images
Provide text alternatives for image maps using area elements with alt
Use figcaption within figure elements for images with visible captions
Provide text alternatives for canvas-rendered content
failures: Missing alt attribute on img elements
Using generic alt text like 'image' or 'photo'
Alt text that does not convey the same information as the image
Using CSS background images for informative content without text alternative
Omitting alt text on image buttons
Failing to update alt text when image content changes dynamically
testing_methods: Automated scan for missing alt attributes
Manual review of alt text quality and accuracy
Screen reader testing to verify announced alternatives
Check decorative images have empty alt or role=presentation
Verify complex images have adequate long descriptions

standards.name: Audio-only and Video-only (Prerecorded)
standards.description: For prerecorded audio-only media, a text transcript is provided. For prerecorded video-only media, either a text alternative or an audio track is provided that presents equivalent information.
standards.intent: Allows people who are deaf or hard of hearing to access audio content through transcripts, and people who are blind or have low vision to access video-only content through audio descriptions or text.
techniques: Provide a text transcript for audio-only content like podcasts
Provide a text description or audio track for video-only content
Link the transcript adjacent to the media player
Ensure transcript includes all spoken content and relevant sound effects
Use the track element with kind=descriptions for video-only content
failures: No transcript provided for audio-only content
No alternative provided for video-only content
Transcript is incomplete or inaccurate
Transcript is not programmatically associated with the media
testing_methods: Verify presence of transcript or text alternative
Compare transcript against actual media content for accuracy
Check transcript is easily discoverable near the media player
Verify transcript includes speaker identification

standards.name: Captions (Prerecorded)
standards.description: Captions are provided for all prerecorded audio content in synchronized media, except when the media is a media alternative for text and is clearly labeled as such.
standards.intent: Enables people who are deaf or hard of hearing to access the auditory information in synchronized media presentations, including dialogue, sound effects, and musical cues.
techniques: Provide closed captions using WebVTT or TTML format
Use the track element with kind=captions for HTML5 video
Include speaker identification in captions
Caption all dialogue and relevant non-speech audio
Synchronize captions with the audio content
Ensure captions do not obscure important visual content
failures: No captions provided for prerecorded video with audio
Captions are auto-generated without review and contain errors
Captions are not synchronized with the audio
Captions omit relevant non-speech sounds
Captions are embedded as open captions with no way to toggle
testing_methods: Verify captions exist for all prerecorded multimedia
Review caption accuracy against actual audio
Check synchronization between captions and audio
Verify non-speech audio is captioned where relevant
Test caption toggle functionality

standards.name: Audio Description or Media Alternative (Prerecorded)
standards.description: An alternative for time-based media or audio description of the prerecorded video content is provided for synchronized media, except when the media is a media alternative for text and is clearly labeled as such.
standards.intent: Provides access to visual information in video for people who are blind or have low vision by describing important visual content not conveyed through existing audio.
techniques: Provide audio descriptions during natural pauses in dialogue
Provide a full text alternative describing both visual and auditory content
Use a second audio track for audio descriptions
Provide extended audio description when pauses are insufficient
failures: No audio description or full text alternative provided
Audio description omits important visual information
Text alternative does not cover key visual content
testing_methods: Verify audio description or text alternative exists
Review that important visual information is described
Check audio description does not conflict with primary audio
Test with screen reader to verify accessibility of text alternative

standards.name: Audio Description (Prerecorded)
standards.description: Audio description is provided for all prerecorded video content in synchronized media.
standards.intent: At Level AA, a full audio description track is required rather than just a text alternative. This ensures blind or low-vision users can follow the visual content of video in real time.
techniques: Provide a separate audio description track for all prerecorded video
Ensure audio descriptions convey all important visual details
Use extended audio description when natural pauses are insufficient
Include a toggle control for enabling audio descriptions
failures: Only providing a text transcript without audio description
Audio description track is incomplete
No mechanism to enable or disable audio description
testing_methods: Verify dedicated audio description track exists
Review completeness of visual content description
Test toggle control for audio description
Confirm audio description does not overlap primary dialogue

standards.name: Info and Relationships
standards.description: Information, structure, and relationships conveyed through presentation can be programmatically determined or are available in text.
standards.intent: Ensures that the structural and relational information implied by visual formatting is also available to assistive technologies so users can understand and navigate content regardless of how it is presented.
techniques: Use semantic HTML elements (h1-h6, p, ul, ol, table, form, fieldset, legend)
Associate form labels with controls using for/id or wrapping
Use thead, tbody, th with scope for data tables
Use fieldset and legend to group related form controls
Use aria-describedby for supplementary descriptions
Mark required fields with aria-required
Use list markup for groups of related links or items
Use landmark roles to identify page regions
failures: Using visual styling alone to convey structure without semantic markup
Using tables for layout instead of CSS
Missing labels on form controls
Using br elements to create visual lists instead of ul/ol
Heading hierarchy is skipped or illogical
Data tables without proper header associations
testing_methods: Automated scan for semantic HTML usage
Check heading hierarchy with browser dev tools
Verify form controls have associated labels
Test table structure with screen reader
Linearize content and verify information is still conveyed
Inspect landmark roles with accessibility tree

standards.name: Meaningful Sequence
standards.description: When the sequence in which content is presented affects its meaning, a correct reading sequence can be programmatically determined.
standards.intent: Ensures that the order of content in the DOM matches the intended reading order so that assistive technologies present content in a meaningful sequence.
techniques: Ensure DOM order matches visual presentation order
Use CSS for visual positioning without altering DOM order
Avoid using tabindex values greater than 0 that disrupt natural order
Use CSS flexbox order property carefully, ensuring DOM order remains logical
Test reading order with CSS disabled
failures: Using CSS to reposition content in a way that changes meaning but DOM order is wrong
Using tabindex to force a reading order that differs from DOM order
Using CSS float or position to visually reorder content without matching DOM
Content injected via JavaScript appears in wrong position
testing_methods: Disable CSS and verify content reads in meaningful order
Use screen reader to navigate content and verify sequence
Check DOM order against visual presentation
Test with browser reading mode

standards.name: Sensory Characteristics
standards.description: Instructions provided for understanding and operating content do not rely solely on sensory characteristics of components such as shape, color, size, visual location, orientation, or sound.
standards.intent: Ensures all users can follow instructions regardless of their ability to perceive shape, size, color, or location. Instructions must include non-sensory information like text labels.
techniques: Include text labels alongside icons or shapes in instructions
Do not rely solely on color to indicate required fields or errors
Provide text-based instructions rather than only 'click the round button'
Use multiple cues (color plus text plus icon) for important states
failures: Instructions like 'press the green button' without additional identifying text
Referring to content only by its visual location ('see the sidebar')
Using only shape references ('click the triangle icon') without text labels
Relying on audio cues alone to signal events
testing_methods: Review all instructional text for sensory-only references
Check that references to interface elements include text labels
Verify error messages do not rely solely on color
Test with monochrome display settings

standards.name: Orientation
standards.description: Content does not restrict its view and operation to a single display orientation, such as portrait or landscape, unless a specific display orientation is essential.
standards.intent: Ensures users can view and interact with content in any orientation. Some users mount devices in fixed positions and cannot rotate them. Content must adapt to both portrait and landscape.
techniques: Use responsive CSS that works in both portrait and landscape
Avoid using orientation-specific media queries that hide content
Test layouts in both orientations
Use flexible grid systems that adapt to available space
failures: Locking the application to portrait or landscape mode
Using orientation-specific CSS that hides or breaks content
JavaScript that forces a particular orientation
Content that only functions correctly in one orientation
testing_methods: Rotate device or emulator and verify all content is accessible
Check for orientation lock in manifest or meta tags
Test responsive layouts in both orientations
Verify no content is lost or obscured when orientation changes

standards.name: Identify Input Purpose
standards.description: The purpose of each input field collecting information about the user can be programmatically determined when the input field serves a purpose identified in the Input Purposes for User Interface Components section.
standards.intent: Enables browsers and assistive technologies to auto-fill form fields and present familiar icons or symbols, reducing cognitive load for users with disabilities and making forms easier to complete.
techniques: Use the autocomplete attribute with appropriate values (name, email, tel, etc.)
Apply autocomplete to fields collecting user information per HTML spec
Map input purposes to WCAG-defined input purpose taxonomy
Use appropriate input types (type=email, type=tel, etc.)
failures: Missing autocomplete attribute on user information fields
Incorrect autocomplete values that do not match field purpose
Using generic autocomplete=on instead of specific tokens
Input fields for personal data without programmatic purpose identification
testing_methods: Automated scan for autocomplete attributes on form fields
Verify autocomplete values match the actual input purpose
Test browser autofill functionality
Check against WCAG input purpose taxonomy

standards.name: Use of Color
standards.description: Color is not used as the only visual means of conveying information, indicating an action, prompting a response, or distinguishing a visual element.
standards.intent: Ensures that users who cannot distinguish between colors, including those with color blindness or who use monochrome displays, can still perceive all information conveyed visually.
techniques: Add text labels or patterns in addition to color coding
Use icons alongside color indicators for status
Underline links in addition to color differentiation
Add patterns or textures to distinguish chart segments
Use shape differences alongside color in data visualization
failures: Using only red/green to indicate valid/invalid form fields
Links distinguishable from body text only by color
Charts or graphs that rely solely on color to differentiate data
Required fields indicated only with a colored border
Status indicators using only color (green=good, red=bad)
testing_methods: View content in grayscale to verify information is still conveyed
Use color blindness simulation tools
Check that all color-coded information has redundant visual cues
Verify links have non-color indicators (underline, icon)
Test data visualizations with color contrast analyzers

standards.name: Audio Control
standards.description: If any audio on a Web page plays automatically for more than 3 seconds, either a mechanism is available to pause or stop the audio, or a mechanism is available to control audio volume independently from the overall system volume level.
standards.intent: Users who use screen readers need to be able to hear the screen reader output without interference from other audio. Auto-playing audio can make it impossible for screen reader users to navigate a page.
techniques: Do not auto-play audio content
Provide a visible control to pause, stop, or mute audio within the first three seconds
Provide independent volume control for page audio
Use autoplay attribute with muted for video elements
failures: Audio plays automatically for more than 3 seconds without a control to stop it
Auto-playing audio without a mute button accessible within three seconds
Background music or ambient sound without volume control
Audio control is not keyboard accessible
testing_methods: Load page and verify no auto-playing audio above 3 seconds
If audio auto-plays, verify pause/stop/mute control exists
Check audio control is keyboard accessible
Verify volume control is independent from system volume

standards.name: Contrast (Minimum)
standards.description: The visual presentation of text and images of text has a contrast ratio of at least 4.5:1, except for large text (at least 3:1), incidental text, logotypes, and pure decoration.
standards.intent: Ensures text is readable for users with moderately low vision who do not use contrast-enhancing assistive technology. The 4.5:1 ratio compensates for the loss in contrast sensitivity typical of users with approximately 20/40 vision.
techniques: Use contrast ratio of at least 4.5:1 for normal text (under 18pt or 14pt bold)
Use contrast ratio of at least 3:1 for large text (18pt+ or 14pt+ bold)
Verify contrast against all background colors including gradients
Provide high-contrast mode or theme option
Use CSS custom properties to enable contrast themes
Test contrast with overlapping elements and transparency
failures: Light gray text on white background below 4.5:1 ratio
Placeholder text with insufficient contrast
Text over images or gradients without ensuring minimum contrast
Disabled state text that is still meant to be read but has low contrast
Link text that fails contrast against surrounding text and background
testing_methods: Use automated contrast checking tools (axe, Lighthouse, WAVE)
Manual check with color contrast analyzers for complex backgrounds
Test text over images and gradients
Verify contrast in all component states (hover, focus, active, disabled)
Check contrast in both light and dark themes

standards.name: Resize Text
standards.description: Except for captions and images of text, text can be resized without assistive technology up to 200 percent without loss of content or functionality.
standards.intent: Ensures that visually rendered text can be scaled by users with low vision to a size they can read, without requiring specialized assistive technology and without content overflow or clipping.
techniques: Use relative units (em, rem, %) for font sizes instead of px
Ensure layouts accommodate text enlargement without overflow
Avoid fixed-height containers that clip enlarged text
Test with browser zoom at 200%
Use responsive design that adapts to text size changes
failures: Text in fixed pixel sizes that cannot be resized
Content clipped or overlapping at 200% zoom
Horizontal scrollbar required at 200% text size in single-column layout
Functionality lost when text is resized
Using viewport units for text size without clamp or min
testing_methods: Zoom browser to 200% and verify no content loss
Check for text overlap or clipping at 200%
Verify all interactive elements remain functional at 200%
Test text resizing in browser settings (not just zoom)
Verify scrollable containers still function

standards.name: Images of Text
standards.description: If the technologies being used can achieve the visual presentation, text is used to convey information rather than images of text, except when the image of text is essential (e.g., logotypes) or can be visually customized to the user's requirements.
standards.intent: Enables users to adjust text presentation (size, color, font) according to their needs. Images of text cannot be resized without degradation and cannot be customized by user stylesheets.
techniques: Use actual text with CSS styling instead of images of text
Use web fonts for custom typography
Use SVG text elements that can be styled and scaled
Reserve images of text only for logos and branding where essential
failures: Using images for headings or navigation text
Embedding text in banner images without real text overlay
Using image-based buttons instead of styled text buttons
Screenshots of text used instead of actual text content
testing_methods: Identify all images containing text
Verify images of text are limited to logos and essential cases
Check that text styling achieves the desired visual effect
Verify text in images can be replaced with actual text

standards.name: Reflow
standards.description: Content can be presented without loss of information or functionality, and without requiring scrolling in two dimensions, for vertical scrolling content at a width equivalent to 320 CSS pixels, and for horizontal scrolling content at a height equivalent to 256 CSS pixels, except for parts of content which require two-dimensional layout for usage or meaning.
standards.intent: Ensures that users who zoom in to 400% or use narrow viewports can read content in a single column without horizontal scrolling. This is essential for low-vision users and users of mobile devices.
techniques: Use responsive design with breakpoints down to 320px width
Avoid horizontal scrolling for text content at narrow widths
Use CSS media queries to adapt layout for small screens
Ensure tables reflow or provide a scrollable wrapper with clear indication
Use CSS grid and flexbox for flexible layouts
Test at 320px viewport width (equivalent to 400% zoom on 1280px)
failures: Content requires horizontal scrolling at 320px width
Fixed-width layouts that do not adapt to narrow viewports
Content hidden or truncated without access at 320px
Two-column layouts that do not collapse to single column
Overflow hidden on containers causing content loss
testing_methods: Set viewport to 320px width and verify content reflows
Zoom browser to 400% on 1280px display
Verify no horizontal scrollbar appears for text content
Check that all content and functionality is preserved
Test data tables for appropriate narrow-screen behavior

standards.name: Non-text Contrast
standards.description: The visual presentation of UI components (their states and boundaries) and graphical objects required to understand content have a contrast ratio of at least 3:1 against adjacent colors.
standards.intent: Ensures that user interface components and meaningful graphics are visible to users with low vision. Applies to component boundaries, focus indicators, icons, and charts.
techniques: Ensure form input borders have at least 3:1 contrast against background
Ensure focus indicators have at least 3:1 contrast
Ensure icons and graphical elements have at least 3:1 contrast
Ensure chart elements have at least 3:1 contrast against adjacent elements
Use visible borders on custom controls like checkboxes and radio buttons
failures: Form input borders with insufficient contrast against page background
Custom checkboxes or toggles with low contrast boundaries
Icons that are too light against their background
Chart segments with insufficient contrast between adjacent elements
Focus indicators that do not meet 3:1 contrast ratio
testing_methods: Use color contrast analyzer on UI component boundaries
Check contrast of all interactive component states
Verify icon contrast against their backgrounds
Test chart and graph element contrast
Verify custom control boundaries are visible

standards.name: Text Spacing
standards.description: No loss of content or functionality occurs when users override text spacing to: line height to at least 1.5 times the font size, spacing following paragraphs to at least 2 times the font size, letter spacing to at least 0.12 times the font size, and word spacing to at least 0.16 times the font size.
standards.intent: Enables users with dyslexia or low vision to increase text spacing to improve readability without content being clipped, truncated, or overlapped.
techniques: Use containers that expand with content when text spacing changes
Avoid fixed-height containers for text content
Use relative units that accommodate spacing overrides
Test with text spacing bookmarklet at WCAG-specified values
Allow CSS overrides for line-height, letter-spacing, word-spacing
failures: Text clipped when line height increased to 1.5x
Content overlapping when letter spacing increased to 0.12em
Container overflow hidden causing content loss with increased spacing
Buttons or labels breaking with increased word spacing
Fixed-height text containers that clip content
testing_methods: Apply WCAG text spacing bookmarklet and verify no content loss
Set line-height: 1.5, letter-spacing: 0.12em, word-spacing: 0.16em
Set paragraph spacing to 2em and verify layout integrity
Check all components for text clipping or overlap
Test in multiple browsers with spacing overrides

standards.name: Content on Hover or Focus
standards.description: Where receiving and then removing pointer hover or keyboard focus triggers additional content to become visible and then hidden, the following are true: the additional content is dismissible, hoverable, and persistent.
standards.intent: Ensures that pop-up content such as tooltips and submenus can be perceived and interacted with by all users, including those using magnification who may need to move their pointer over the pop-up content.
techniques: Make hover/focus-triggered content dismissible via Escape key
Allow pointer to move over the triggered content without it disappearing
Keep content visible until hover/focus is explicitly removed or dismissed
Use aria-describedby or aria-details instead of tooltips for complex info
Ensure triggered content does not obscure other content without dismissal option
failures: Tooltip disappears when user moves pointer toward it
No way to dismiss hover content without moving pointer
Content disappears on a timer before user can read it
Hover content obscures the trigger element or other content
Focus-triggered content not keyboard dismissible
testing_methods: Hover over elements and verify additional content is persistent
Move pointer from trigger to hover content and verify it remains
Press Escape and verify hover content is dismissed
Tab to trigger elements and verify focus-triggered content behavior
Test with screen magnifier to verify content is reachable

standards.name: Keyboard
standards.description: All functionality of the content is operable through a keyboard interface without requiring specific timings for individual keystrokes, except where the underlying function requires input that depends on the path of the user's movement and not just the endpoints.
standards.intent: Ensures that users who rely on keyboards, switch devices, voice input, or other non-pointer input methods can access all interactive functionality.
techniques: Use native HTML interactive elements (button, a, input, select)
Add tabindex=0 and keyboard event handlers to custom interactive elements
Implement Enter and Space key activation for custom buttons
Implement arrow key navigation for custom widgets (tabs, menus, trees)
Ensure drag-and-drop has keyboard alternatives
Provide keyboard shortcuts for common actions
failures: Interactive elements only respond to mouse click events
Custom controls missing keyboard event handlers
Using div or span as interactive elements without keyboard support
Drag-and-drop functionality with no keyboard alternative
On-screen drawing tools with no keyboard alternative (may be exempt)
testing_methods: Navigate entire page using only Tab, Shift+Tab, Enter, Space, and arrow keys
Verify all interactive elements are reachable via keyboard
Test all functionality can be completed without a mouse
Check custom widgets implement expected keyboard patterns
Verify keyboard shortcuts do not conflict with assistive technology

standards.name: No Keyboard Trap
standards.description: If keyboard focus can be moved to a component of the page using a keyboard interface, then focus can be moved away from that component using only a keyboard interface, and if it requires more than unmodified arrow or tab keys, the user is advised of the method for moving focus.
standards.intent: Prevents users from becoming trapped in a component with no way to exit using the keyboard alone, which would render the rest of the page inaccessible to keyboard users.
techniques: Ensure Tab and Shift+Tab move focus through all interactive elements
Implement Escape key to close modal dialogs and return focus
Provide clear instructions if non-standard keys are needed to exit a component
Test focus management in embedded content (iframes, plugins)
Ensure focus is not trapped in auto-complete dropdowns or date pickers
failures: Modal dialog that cannot be closed with Escape key
Embedded widget that traps keyboard focus
Tab key cycles within a component with no exit
Rich text editors that capture all keyboard input
Video player controls that trap focus
testing_methods: Tab through all interactive elements and verify focus can always move forward and backward
Enter all modal dialogs and verify Escape exits them
Check embedded content (iframes) for focus traps
Test custom widgets for keyboard exit capability
Verify focus returns to a logical element after closing overlays

standards.name: Character Key Shortcuts
standards.description: If a keyboard shortcut is implemented using only letter, punctuation, number, or symbol characters, then at least one of the following is true: the shortcut can be turned off, remapped, or is only active when the relevant UI component has focus.
standards.intent: Prevents single-character keyboard shortcuts from interfering with speech input users who may inadvertently trigger shortcuts when dictating text, and power users who want to customize shortcuts.
techniques: Require modifier keys (Ctrl, Alt, Cmd) for keyboard shortcuts
Provide a settings panel to disable or remap shortcuts
Scope single-character shortcuts to specific focused components only
Document all keyboard shortcuts and their customization options
failures: Single letter shortcuts active globally without modifier keys
No mechanism to turn off or remap single-character shortcuts
Speech input triggers unintended actions via character shortcuts
Shortcuts conflict with browser or assistive technology shortcuts
testing_methods: Identify all keyboard shortcuts in the application
Verify single-character shortcuts can be turned off or remapped
Test with speech recognition software
Verify shortcuts are only active when relevant component is focused
Check for conflicts with common assistive technology shortcuts

standards.name: Timing Adjustable
standards.description: For each time limit that is set by the content, the user can turn off, adjust, or extend the time limit, except for real-time events, essential time limits, and time limits longer than 20 hours.
standards.intent: Provides users with disabilities, who may need more time to read or interact with content, the ability to complete tasks without arbitrary time constraints.
techniques: Provide a warning before time expires with option to extend
Allow users to set their own time limits in preferences
Provide at least 10 times the default time limit option
Allow at least 20 seconds to extend time with a simple action
Remove time limits when they are not essential
failures: Session timeout without warning or extension option
Auto-advancing carousel with no pause or timing control
Form that times out and loses user data
Timed quiz with no accommodation for extended time
Auto-redirect after fixed time with no user control
testing_methods: Identify all time-limited interactions
Verify warning is provided before timeout
Test extension mechanism is accessible and functional
Verify data is preserved when time is extended
Check that timing can be adjusted in user preferences

standards.name: Pause, Stop, Hide
standards.description: For moving, blinking, scrolling, or auto-updating information: if it starts automatically, lasts more than 5 seconds, and is presented in parallel with other content, there is a mechanism to pause, stop, or hide it. For auto-updating information, there is a mechanism to pause, stop, hide, or control the frequency.
standards.intent: Ensures that users with attention deficits or cognitive disabilities are not distracted by moving content, and that users who need more time can pause or stop animations to read content.
techniques: Provide pause/stop controls for carousels and slideshows
Honor prefers-reduced-motion media query
Provide stop controls for auto-scrolling or marquee content
Allow users to control update frequency of live data
Use CSS animation-play-state for pausable animations
failures: Auto-playing carousel with no pause button
Animated background that cannot be stopped
Auto-scrolling text with no stop mechanism
Blinking or flashing content lasting more than 5 seconds
Live updating content with no pause or frequency control
testing_methods: Identify all moving, blinking, scrolling, or auto-updating content
Verify pause/stop/hide controls exist and are accessible
Test with prefers-reduced-motion enabled
Verify pausing preserves content state
Check auto-updating content for frequency controls

standards.name: Three Flashes or Below Threshold
standards.description: Web pages do not contain anything that flashes more than three times in any one second period, or the flash is below the general flash and red flash thresholds.
standards.intent: Protects users with photosensitive epilepsy from seizures that can be triggered by flashing content. Even brief exposure to flashing content can cause life-threatening seizures.
techniques: Avoid content that flashes more than 3 times per second
Ensure any flashing content is below the general flash threshold
Avoid large areas of flashing content
Use the Photosensitive Epilepsy Analysis Tool (PEAT) to test content
Provide a warning before displaying potentially hazardous content
failures: Animations flashing more than 3 times per second
Video content with rapid strobing effects
CSS animations creating rapid flicker effects
Large red flashing areas exceeding threshold
testing_methods: Use PEAT or Harding FPA to analyze video content
Manually count flash frequency in animations
Check CSS animations for rapid opacity or color changes
Verify flash area is small relative to viewport
Test with photosensitive seizure analysis tools

standards.name: Bypass Blocks
standards.description: A mechanism is available to bypass blocks of content that are repeated on multiple Web pages.
standards.intent: Allows keyboard users and screen reader users to skip past repeated content like navigation menus and headers to reach the main content quickly.
techniques: Provide a 'skip to main content' link as the first focusable element
Use ARIA landmark roles to define page regions
Use heading elements to structure content for easy navigation
Provide a table of contents for long pages
Use HTML5 structural elements (nav, main, aside, header, footer)
failures: No skip navigation link provided
Skip link is present but not functional
Skip link target does not receive focus
No landmark roles or structural elements to enable bypass
Skip link is permanently hidden and not accessible
testing_methods: Tab to first element on page and verify skip link appears
Activate skip link and verify focus moves to main content
Test with screen reader landmark navigation
Verify heading structure enables content bypass
Check skip link visibility on focus

standards.name: Page Titled
standards.description: Web pages have titles that describe topic or purpose.
standards.intent: Helps users orient themselves and identify the page content without having to read the full page. Page titles are displayed in browser tabs, bookmarks, and search results, and are the first element announced by screen readers.
techniques: Provide a descriptive title element in the head of each page
Include the site name and page-specific information in the title
Update page title dynamically for single-page applications
Use a consistent title format across the site (e.g., 'Page Name - Site Name')
Ensure title is unique for each page
failures: Missing title element
Generic title like 'Untitled' or 'Page'
Same title used for all pages
Title does not describe the page content
SPA does not update title on route change
testing_methods: Check title element exists in page head
Verify title accurately describes page content
Check title uniqueness across pages
Verify SPA updates title on navigation
Test title announcement with screen reader

standards.name: Focus Order
standards.description: If a Web page can be navigated sequentially and the navigation sequences affect meaning or operation, focusable components receive focus in an order that preserves meaning and operability.
standards.intent: Ensures keyboard users encounter interactive elements in a logical order that matches the visual layout and expected workflow, so they can understand and operate the interface effectively.
techniques: Ensure DOM order matches visual layout order
Avoid positive tabindex values that override natural order
Manage focus programmatically when content is dynamically added
Return focus to trigger element when closing modals or dialogs
Use focus management for multi-step workflows
failures: Using tabindex values greater than 0
Focus order does not match visual layout
Focus lost when content is dynamically shown or hidden
Focus not returned to trigger after modal closes
Focus order skips important interactive elements
testing_methods: Tab through all interactive elements and verify logical order
Compare tab order with visual layout
Test dynamic content for proper focus management
Verify focus returns correctly after closing overlays
Check for tabindex values greater than 0

standards.name: Link Purpose (In Context)
standards.description: The purpose of each link can be determined from the link text alone or from the link text together with its programmatically determined link context, except where the purpose of the link would be ambiguous to users in general.
standards.intent: Allows users, especially those using screen readers who may navigate by links, to understand the destination or purpose of each link without needing to read surrounding content.
techniques: Use descriptive link text that indicates the destination
Add aria-label or aria-labelledby for links with generic text
Provide context through the enclosing sentence, paragraph, or list item
Use aria-describedby for supplementary link context
Ensure link text is unique when links go to different destinations
failures: Links with text like 'click here', 'read more', 'learn more' without context
Multiple 'Read more' links on a page without distinguishing context
Links with the same text going to different destinations
Empty links with no text or aria-label
Image links without alt text
testing_methods: List all links on the page and verify purpose is determinable
Screen reader testing of link text in isolation
Check for generic link text without programmatic context
Verify image links have appropriate alt text
Test links list in screen reader for distinguishability

standards.name: Multiple Ways
standards.description: More than one way is available to locate a Web page within a set of Web pages except where the Web Page is the result of, or a step in, a process.
standards.intent: Enables users to find content using the method that best suits their needs, whether through navigation menus, search, site maps, links, or other mechanisms.
techniques: Provide a site search function
Provide a site map or table of contents
Provide a consistent navigation menu
Provide breadcrumb navigation
Provide related links between pages
failures: Only one method to navigate to pages (e.g., only main menu)
No search functionality on a large site
No site map and only sequential navigation
Search function that is not accessible
testing_methods: Verify at least two navigation methods exist
Test search functionality
Check for site map or index
Verify breadcrumb navigation where applicable
Test navigation methods are accessible

standards.name: Headings and Labels
standards.description: Headings and labels describe topic or purpose.
standards.intent: Helps users understand the organization of content and the purpose of form controls. Descriptive headings and labels enable efficient navigation and reduce errors.
techniques: Use descriptive heading text that summarizes the following content
Use unique headings for distinct sections
Provide descriptive labels for all form controls
Ensure labels clearly indicate what input is expected
Use legend elements for fieldsets to provide group labels
failures: Generic headings like 'Section 1' that do not describe content
Form labels that do not clearly describe expected input
Missing labels on form groups
Heading text that duplicates across unrelated sections
Labels that use jargon or abbreviations without explanation
testing_methods: Review all headings for descriptive accuracy
Verify form labels clearly describe expected input
Test heading navigation with screen reader
Check fieldset legend descriptions
Verify labels match the purpose of their controls

standards.name: Focus Visible
standards.description: Any keyboard operable user interface has a mode of operation where the keyboard focus indicator is visible.
standards.intent: Ensures keyboard users can always see which element currently has focus, enabling them to track their position on the page and interact with the correct element.
techniques: Maintain default browser focus outlines or provide custom visible focus styles
Use :focus-visible pseudo-class for keyboard-only focus styles
Ensure focus indicator has sufficient contrast (meets 1.4.11)
Use outline, box-shadow, or border for focus indication
Ensure focus indicator is visible on all backgrounds
failures: Removing focus outlines with outline: none without replacement
Focus indicator with insufficient contrast against background
Focus indicator hidden behind other elements
Custom focus styles that are not visible on all backgrounds
Focus indicator only visible on some interactive elements
testing_methods: Tab through all interactive elements and verify focus is visible
Check focus visibility on different background colors
Verify custom focus styles meet contrast requirements
Test focus visibility in high contrast mode
Check that :focus-visible is used appropriately

standards.name: Focus Not Obscured (Minimum)
standards.description: When a user interface component receives keyboard focus, the component is not entirely hidden due to author-created content.
standards.intent: Ensures that when keyboard focus moves to an element, it is at least partially visible and not completely obscured by sticky headers, footers, overlays, or other positioned content.
techniques: Use scroll-padding to account for sticky headers and footers
Ensure focused elements are scrolled into view when behind fixed elements
Manage z-index to prevent overlays from obscuring focused elements
Use scroll-margin on focusable elements near sticky regions
Test focus visibility with all persistent UI elements present
failures: Focused element hidden behind a sticky header or footer
Cookie banner obscuring focused elements
Chat widget overlaying focused form fields
Sticky navigation covering focused elements when scrolling
Modal backdrop remaining and covering focused elements
testing_methods: Tab through page with sticky headers/footers and verify focus is visible
Check focus visibility behind cookie banners and chat widgets
Test focus at the boundaries of scrollable regions
Verify focused elements near sticky elements are at least partially visible
Test with various viewport sizes

standards.name: Pointer Gestures
standards.description: All functionality that uses multipoint or path-based gestures for operation can be operated with a single pointer without a path-based gesture, unless a multipoint or path-based gesture is essential.
standards.intent: Ensures that users who cannot perform complex gestures like pinch-zoom, multi-finger swipes, or path-based gestures can still operate all functionality using simple single-point activation.
techniques: Provide button controls as alternatives to pinch-zoom gestures
Provide single-tap alternatives to swipe gestures
Provide button controls for path-based drawing actions
Ensure carousel navigation has next/previous buttons in addition to swipe
Provide zoom in/out buttons for map interfaces
failures: Map zooming only available through pinch gesture
Carousel only navigable by swiping
Drawing tools requiring path-based gestures with no alternative
Multi-finger gestures required with no single-pointer alternative
testing_methods: Identify all multipoint and path-based gesture interactions
Verify single-pointer alternatives exist for each gesture
Test all functionality using only single-point clicks/taps
Verify alternative controls are discoverable and accessible
Test with assistive technology that simulates single-point interaction

standards.name: Pointer Cancellation
standards.description: For functionality that can be operated using a single pointer, at least one of the following is true: the down-event is not used, the action is executed on the up-event with an abort/undo mechanism, the up-event reverses the down-event, or completing the function on the down-event is essential.
standards.intent: Makes it easier for users to avoid accidental activation of controls by providing the ability to cancel or undo an action. Users with motor impairments are especially prone to accidental touches.
techniques: Execute actions on the up-event (mouseup, pointerup, touchend) rather than down-event
Use native HTML elements that inherently use up-event activation
Provide an undo mechanism for actions triggered on down-event
Allow users to move pointer away from target before releasing to cancel
Use click event which fires on release by default
failures: Actions triggered on mousedown or touchstart with no cancel option
Custom buttons that fire on pointer down without release confirmation
Drag operations that commit on the initial touch
No ability to abort by moving pointer away before release
testing_methods: Click and hold on interactive elements, then move pointer away before releasing
Verify action is not triggered if pointer is released outside the target
Test touch interactions for accidental activation prevention
Check that native click behavior is preserved
Verify drag operations can be cancelled

standards.name: Label in Name
standards.description: For user interface components with labels that include text or images of text, the name contains the text that is presented visually.
standards.intent: Ensures that users who rely on speech input can activate controls by speaking the visible label text. If the accessible name does not contain the visible label, speech input users cannot activate the control by speaking what they see.
techniques: Ensure aria-label includes the visible text label
Match the start of aria-label with the visible label text
Use aria-labelledby to reference the visible label element
Avoid aria-label that contradicts or omits the visible text
Ensure icon buttons with visible text have matching accessible names
failures: Visible label says 'Search' but aria-label says 'Find products'
Button text says 'Submit' but accessible name is 'Send form data'
Image alt text does not match adjacent visible text
aria-label completely replaces visible label with different text
testing_methods: Compare visible labels with accessible names in accessibility tree
Verify aria-label contains visible text
Test with speech recognition software by speaking visible labels
Use accessibility inspector to compare visible and accessible names
Automated scan for mismatches between visible and accessible names

standards.name: Motion Actuation
standards.description: Functionality that can be operated by device motion or user motion can also be operated by user interface components and responding to the motion can be disabled to prevent accidental actuation, except when the motion is essential or disabling would invalidate the activity.
standards.intent: Ensures that users who cannot perform device motions (such as shaking or tilting) due to their device being mounted or due to motor impairments can still access all functionality through alternative controls.
techniques: Provide button controls as alternatives to shake-to-undo
Provide UI controls for tilt-based interactions
Allow users to disable motion-based controls in settings
Provide on-screen alternatives for all motion-triggered actions
failures: Shake to undo with no button alternative
Tilt-to-scroll with no scrollbar or button alternative
Motion-based gaming controls with no alternative input
No option to disable motion actuation
testing_methods: Identify all motion-activated functionality
Verify UI alternatives exist for each motion action
Check settings for motion actuation disable option
Test all motion-based features with device stationary
Verify alternative controls are accessible

standards.name: Dragging Movements
standards.description: All functionality that uses a dragging movement for operation can be achieved by a single pointer without dragging, unless dragging is essential or the functionality is determined by the user agent and not modified by the author.
standards.intent: Ensures that users who cannot perform drag operations due to motor impairments can still access all functionality through single-pointer actions like clicks or taps.
techniques: Provide click-to-select and click-to-place as alternative to drag-and-drop
Provide up/down buttons for reordering list items
Provide input fields for setting slider values
Allow click-based selection for sortable interfaces
Provide context menu options as alternatives to drag actions
failures: Drag-and-drop reordering with no alternative mechanism
Slider only operable through dragging
File upload only through drag-and-drop
Kanban board items only movable by dragging
testing_methods: Identify all drag-based interactions
Verify single-pointer alternatives exist
Test alternatives are accessible and functional
Verify keyboard alternatives also exist
Test with assistive technology that does not support drag

standards.name: Target Size (Minimum)
standards.description: The size of the target for pointer inputs is at least 24 by 24 CSS pixels, except when the target is in a sentence or block of text, the target has an equivalent control meeting the size, the target is determined by the user agent, or the target size is essential.
standards.intent: Ensures that interactive targets are large enough for users with motor impairments to activate accurately without accidentally hitting adjacent targets.
techniques: Ensure all clickable/tappable elements are at least 24x24px
Use padding to increase touch target size for small visual elements
Provide adequate spacing between adjacent targets
Use min-width and min-height for interactive elements
Ensure inline links in text have adequate touch area through line-height
failures: Icon buttons smaller than 24x24px
Closely spaced links or buttons with targets under 24x24px
Small checkbox or radio button targets
Close buttons or dismiss controls under 24x24px
Pagination links too small and too closely spaced
testing_methods: Measure target sizes using browser dev tools
Verify 24x24px minimum for all interactive elements
Check spacing between adjacent targets
Test touch accuracy on mobile devices
Automated scan for undersized interactive elements

standards.name: Language of Page
standards.description: The default human language of each Web page can be programmatically determined.
standards.intent: Enables screen readers to use correct pronunciation rules, search engines to index properly, and browsers to display correct character sets by specifying the page language.
techniques: Set the lang attribute on the html element
Use valid IANA language subtag (e.g., en, fr, de, zh-Hans)
Ensure lang attribute matches the primary language of the content
Update lang attribute for single-page applications when language changes
failures: Missing lang attribute on the html element
Invalid or incorrect lang attribute value
lang attribute does not match the actual content language
SPA does not update lang on language change
testing_methods: Automated scan for lang attribute on html element
Verify lang value matches content language
Validate lang attribute against IANA subtag registry
Test screen reader pronunciation with different lang values

standards.name: Language of Parts
standards.description: The human language of each passage or phrase in the content can be programmatically determined except for proper names, technical terms, words of indeterminate language, and words or phrases that have become part of the vernacular of the immediately surrounding text.
standards.intent: Ensures screen readers switch pronunciation rules for content in different languages, so users hear correct pronunciation for foreign language passages.
techniques: Use the lang attribute on elements containing text in a different language
Apply lang to blockquote, span, or other elements wrapping foreign text
Use valid IANA language subtags for language identification
Mark up language changes in multilingual content
failures: Foreign language passages not marked with lang attribute
Incorrect lang attribute on foreign language content
Large blocks of text in different language without lang marking
Automated translation without updating lang attributes
testing_methods: Identify foreign language content and verify lang attributes
Test screen reader pronunciation of foreign language passages
Automated scan for unmatched language content
Review multilingual content for proper language marking

standards.name: On Focus
standards.description: When any user interface component receives focus, it does not initiate a change of context.
standards.intent: Ensures that simply navigating to a component does not trigger unexpected changes like form submission, page navigation, or opening new windows, which would disorient users.
techniques: Do not trigger form submission when a field receives focus
Do not navigate to a new page when an element receives focus
Do not open new windows or dialogs on focus
Use activation events (click, Enter) for context changes, not focus events
failures: New window opens when a link receives focus
Form submits when focus moves to submit button
Dropdown navigation that navigates on focus
Modal dialog opens when an element receives focus
testing_methods: Tab through all interactive elements and verify no context change
Check for onfocus event handlers that trigger navigation or submission
Verify no new windows open on focus
Test dynamic content changes that occur on focus

standards.name: On Input
standards.description: Changing the setting of any user interface component does not automatically cause a change of context unless the user has been advised of the behavior before using the component.
standards.intent: Ensures that users are not surprised by automatic context changes when interacting with form controls, which is especially problematic for users of screen readers and those with cognitive disabilities.
techniques: Use a submit button rather than auto-submitting on input change
Advise users before components that trigger context changes
Provide a 'Go' or 'Apply' button for filter controls
Use aria-live to announce changes instead of navigating
Describe auto-submit behavior in form instructions
failures: Select dropdown that navigates to a new page on change
Checkbox that opens a new window when checked
Radio button that submits a form automatically
Filter controls that reload the page without warning
testing_methods: Interact with all form controls and verify no unexpected context changes
Check for onchange handlers that trigger navigation
Verify advance notification for controls that cause context changes
Test with screen reader for unexpected announcements on input

standards.name: Consistent Navigation
standards.description: Navigational mechanisms that are repeated on multiple Web pages within a set of Web pages occur in the same relative order each time they are repeated, unless a change is initiated by the user.
standards.intent: Helps users who rely on familiarity and spatial memory to locate navigation elements efficiently by keeping navigation in a consistent position and order across pages.
techniques: Maintain consistent order of navigation items across all pages
Use consistent layout templates for navigation
Keep primary navigation in the same position on every page
Preserve relative order when adding or removing navigation items
Use consistent placement for search, login, and utility navigation
failures: Navigation items reordered on different pages
Primary navigation moved to different positions on different pages
Inconsistent placement of search or utility navigation
Sub-navigation that changes order based on page context without user action
testing_methods: Compare navigation order across multiple pages
Verify navigation position is consistent
Check utility navigation consistency
Test with screen reader across pages for consistent landmark structure

standards.name: Consistent Identification
standards.description: Components that have the same functionality within a set of Web pages are identified consistently.
standards.intent: Reduces cognitive load by ensuring that components with the same function use the same labels, icons, and naming conventions throughout the site, making the interface predictable for all users.
techniques: Use consistent labels for components with the same function
Use consistent icons for the same actions across pages
Maintain consistent accessible names for repeated components
Use consistent alt text for icons that serve the same purpose
Apply design system patterns consistently
failures: Search labeled 'Search' on one page and 'Find' on another
Different icons for the same action on different pages
Inconsistent button labels for the same function
Print icon with different alt text on different pages
testing_methods: Compare labels and names of similar components across pages
Verify consistent icon usage for same functions
Check accessible names for consistency
Review design system component usage across pages

standards.name: Error Identification
standards.description: If an input error is automatically detected, the item that is in error is identified and the error is described to the user in text.
standards.intent: Ensures that users are clearly informed about errors so they can understand what went wrong and take corrective action, particularly important for users with visual or cognitive disabilities.
techniques: Display error messages in text near the invalid field
Use aria-invalid to programmatically mark fields with errors
Use aria-describedby to associate error messages with fields
Provide an error summary at the top of the form
Use role=alert or aria-live for dynamically displayed errors
Move focus to the first error or error summary on submission
failures: Only using color to indicate errors without text
Error message that does not identify which field is in error
Errors indicated only by icons without text description
Error messages not associated with their respective fields
Generic error message like 'Please fix errors' without specifics
testing_methods: Submit form with errors and verify error identification
Check error messages describe the specific error
Verify aria-invalid is set on error fields
Test error message association with screen reader
Verify error summary identifies all errors

standards.name: Labels or Instructions
standards.description: Labels or instructions are provided when content requires user input.
standards.intent: Helps users understand what input is expected for form controls, reducing errors and frustration, especially for users with cognitive disabilities.
techniques: Provide visible labels for all form controls
Use label elements associated with form controls
Provide instructions for complex input formats
Indicate required fields clearly with text (not just asterisk)
Provide placeholder text as supplementary hint (not replacement for label)
Group related fields with fieldset and legend
failures: Input fields with no visible label
Using placeholder text as the only label
No instructions for expected input format (date, phone)
Required fields indicated only by asterisk with no legend
Labels not programmatically associated with controls
testing_methods: Verify all form controls have visible labels
Check label association in accessibility tree
Verify format instructions for specialized inputs
Check required field indicators include text explanation
Test with screen reader for label announcements

standards.name: Error Suggestion
standards.description: If an input error is automatically detected and suggestions for correction are known, then the suggestions are provided to the user, unless it would jeopardize the security or purpose of the content.
standards.intent: Helps users correct errors by providing specific suggestions rather than just identifying that an error occurred, reducing frustration and enabling successful task completion.
techniques: Suggest correct format when input does not match expected pattern
Suggest valid values when input is not in the allowed set
Provide examples of valid input in error messages
Offer 'Did you mean?' suggestions for likely typos
Show expected format alongside error message
failures: Error says 'Invalid date' without suggesting correct format
Error says 'Invalid email' without describing valid format
No suggestion when close match exists in valid options
Generic error messages without corrective guidance
testing_methods: Submit forms with various invalid inputs and check for suggestions
Verify error messages include corrective guidance
Test with common input mistakes to verify helpful suggestions
Check that suggestions do not expose sensitive information
Verify suggestions are accessible to screen readers

standards.name: Error Prevention (Legal, Financial, Data)
standards.description: For Web pages that cause legal commitments or financial transactions, that modify or delete user-controllable data, or that submit test responses: submissions are reversible, data is checked and the user is provided an opportunity to correct, or a mechanism is available to review, confirm, and correct before finalizing.
standards.intent: Protects users from serious consequences of input errors in important transactions by ensuring they can review, correct, or reverse their submissions.
techniques: Provide a confirmation page before final submission
Allow users to review and edit data before submission
Provide an undo or reversal mechanism after submission
Validate all data and present errors before allowing submission
Use a multi-step checkout with review step
failures: Financial transaction processed without confirmation step
Data deleted without confirmation dialog
No way to undo or reverse a submitted action
Test responses submitted without review option
Account deletion without confirmation
testing_methods: Test financial transaction flow for confirmation step
Verify data deletion includes confirmation
Check for undo or reversal mechanisms
Verify review step in multi-step processes
Test that errors are caught before final submission

standards.name: Redundant Entry
standards.description: Information previously entered by or provided to the user that is required to be entered again in the same process is either auto-populated or available for the user to select, unless re-entering the information is essential, the information is required to ensure security, or previously entered information is no longer valid.
standards.intent: Reduces cognitive load and effort for users who may struggle to remember or re-enter information they have already provided, particularly benefiting users with cognitive disabilities and motor impairments.
techniques: Auto-populate fields with previously entered information
Provide a select option to reuse previously entered data
Pre-fill shipping address from billing address with user confirmation
Carry forward user information between process steps
Use session storage to retain form data across steps
failures: Requiring users to re-enter their name on multiple form pages
Not carrying forward address information between billing and shipping
Multi-step form that clears previous entries on back navigation
Requiring re-entry of information already on file
testing_methods: Complete multi-step processes and check for redundant entry requests
Verify previously entered data is auto-populated or selectable
Test back navigation in multi-step forms for data persistence
Check shipping/billing address forms for auto-fill
Verify essential re-entry is limited to security-critical fields

standards.name: Accessible Authentication (Minimum)
standards.description: A cognitive function test (such as remembering a password or solving a puzzle) is not required for any step in an authentication process unless that step provides at least one of: an alternative method, a mechanism to assist the user, or object recognition/personal content tests.
standards.intent: Ensures that users with cognitive disabilities can authenticate without being blocked by tests that require memory, transcription, or puzzle-solving abilities.
techniques: Support password managers and browser autofill for credentials
Provide passkey or WebAuthn authentication
Allow email or SMS magic link authentication
Support OAuth or social login as an alternative
Provide copy-paste support in authentication fields
Use object recognition CAPTCHA rather than text-based
failures: Blocking paste in password fields preventing password manager use
CAPTCHA requiring text transcription with no accessible alternative
Custom input widgets that prevent autofill
Authentication requiring memorization of arbitrary codes
Time-limited OTP entry with no way to paste
testing_methods: Test authentication flow with password manager
Verify paste is allowed in credential fields
Check for alternative authentication methods
Test CAPTCHA for accessible alternatives
Verify autofill works on login forms
Test with assistive technology through full authentication flow

standards.name: Name, Role, Value
standards.description: For all user interface components (including but not limited to: form elements, links, and components generated by scripts), the name and role can be programmatically determined; states, properties, and values that can be set by the user can be programmatically set; and notification of changes to these items is available to user agents, including assistive technologies.
standards.intent: Ensures that assistive technologies can correctly identify and interact with all interface components by providing complete programmatic information about each component's name, role, state, and value.
techniques: Use native HTML elements that have built-in roles and names
Add appropriate ARIA roles to custom components
Provide accessible names via label, aria-label, or aria-labelledby
Use aria-expanded, aria-selected, aria-checked for state communication
Update ARIA states dynamically when component state changes
Use aria-valuenow, aria-valuemin, aria-valuemax for range inputs
failures: Custom button without role=button
Interactive div without accessible name or role
Checkbox state not communicated via aria-checked
Accordion without aria-expanded state
Custom select without proper role and state management
Dynamic state changes not reflected in ARIA attributes
testing_methods: Inspect accessibility tree for correct name, role, and value
Verify ARIA states update dynamically with user interaction
Test custom components with screen reader for correct announcements
Automated scan for missing roles and names
Verify native HTML elements are used where possible
Check custom widgets against ARIA authoring practices

standards.name: Status Messages
standards.description: In content implemented using markup languages, status messages can be programmatically determined through role or properties such that they can be presented to the user by assistive technologies without receiving focus.
standards.intent: Ensures that important status updates like success messages, error counts, search results, and progress indicators are announced to screen reader users without disrupting their current focus position.
techniques: Use role=status for advisory information like success messages
Use role=alert for important time-sensitive messages
Use role=log for chat messages or sequential updates
Use role=progressbar with aria-valuenow for progress updates
Use aria-live=polite for non-urgent status updates
Use aria-live=assertive sparingly for critical updates
failures: Success message displayed visually but not announced to screen readers
Search results count updated without aria-live
Loading indicator not communicated to assistive technologies
Error count in form not announced on update
Toast notification not using appropriate live region role
testing_methods: Test status messages with screen reader for announcements
Verify role=status on success and informational messages
Check aria-live regions for appropriate politeness settings
Test loading and progress indicators for screen reader output
Verify search results count is announced
Check toast notifications use appropriate roles

standards.name: ARIA Landmark Roles
standards.description: ARIA landmark roles identify regions of a page to support navigation by assistive technologies. Key landmarks include banner, navigation, main, contentinfo, complementary, search, form, and region.
standards.intent: Enables screen reader users to quickly navigate to major sections of a page by jumping between landmarks, significantly improving navigation efficiency on complex pages.
techniques: Use HTML5 elements that implicitly provide landmarks (header=banner, nav=navigation, main=main, footer=contentinfo, aside=complementary)
Add role=search to search form containers
Use aria-label or aria-labelledby to distinguish multiple landmarks of the same type
Ensure every page has exactly one main landmark
Use role=region with aria-label for other significant sections
Nest landmarks logically (e.g., navigation within banner)
failures: Page with no landmark roles defined
Multiple nav elements without distinguishing labels
Missing main landmark
Content outside any landmark region
Multiple banner or contentinfo landmarks at top level
Using role=banner or role=contentinfo inside sectioning elements
testing_methods: Use screen reader landmark navigation to verify coverage
Check accessibility tree for landmark structure
Verify unique labels on repeated landmark types
Ensure all page content is within a landmark
Automated scan for landmark role usage
Verify HTML5 element to landmark mapping

standards.name: ARIA Live Regions
standards.description: ARIA live regions announce dynamic content changes to assistive technologies without requiring focus to move. The aria-live attribute accepts values: off (default), polite (wait for idle), and assertive (announce immediately). Related attributes include aria-atomic, aria-relevant, and aria-busy.
standards.intent: Ensures that dynamically updated content is announced to screen reader users who might otherwise miss visual changes, including status updates, chat messages, error notifications, and real-time data.
techniques: Use aria-live=polite for non-urgent updates like search result counts
Use aria-live=assertive only for time-critical information like errors
Use aria-atomic=true when the entire region should be re-announced
Use aria-relevant to control which changes are announced (additions, removals, text)
Set aria-busy=true while loading, false when complete
Place aria-live on the container before content is added dynamically
Use role=status as shorthand for aria-live=polite + aria-atomic=true
Use role=alert as shorthand for aria-live=assertive
failures: Dynamic content updates without aria-live
Overusing aria-live=assertive causing excessive interruptions
Adding aria-live to element after content is already present
Live region that re-announces unchanged content
aria-atomic not set when partial updates are confusing
Too many simultaneous live region updates
testing_methods: Test with screen reader to verify announcements on dynamic updates
Verify politeness level is appropriate for content urgency
Check aria-atomic settings for correct announcement scope
Test aria-busy during loading states
Verify live region exists in DOM before dynamic content is added
Test with multiple simultaneous updates

standards.name: ARIA Widget Roles
standards.description: ARIA widget roles define the type of interactive component for assistive technologies. Common widget roles include button, checkbox, dialog, menubar, menu, menuitem, option, progressbar, radio, radiogroup, slider, switch, tab, tabpanel, tablist, textbox, tree, treeitem, combobox, grid, gridcell, listbox, scrollbar, searchbox, separator, spinbutton, toolbar, and tooltip.
standards.intent: Communicates the expected behavior of custom interactive components to assistive technologies so users understand how to interact with them, what states they can have, and what keyboard patterns to expect.
techniques: Apply the most specific widget role that matches the component behavior
Combine roles with required ARIA states and properties
Use role=button with tabindex=0 and keyboard handlers for custom buttons
Use role=dialog with aria-modal=true and aria-labelledby for modals
Use role=tablist, role=tab, and role=tabpanel for tab interfaces
Use role=combobox with aria-expanded and aria-autocomplete
Use role=switch for toggle switches with aria-checked
Use role=grid with role=row and role=gridcell for data grids
failures: Custom interactive components with no role
Incorrect role assignment (e.g., role=button on a link that navigates)
Widget role without required ARIA states
Role used without implementing expected keyboard behavior
Mixing incompatible roles and properties
Using abstract roles (widget, composite) directly
testing_methods: Verify each custom component has the correct widget role
Check required ARIA states and properties are present
Test keyboard interaction matches expected patterns for the role
Verify screen reader announcements match the widget role
Validate against ARIA authoring practices guide
Automated scan for missing or incorrect roles

standards.name: ARIA States and Properties
standards.description: ARIA states and properties communicate the current condition and characteristics of interactive components to assistive technologies. Key states include aria-expanded, aria-selected, aria-checked, aria-pressed, aria-disabled, aria-hidden, aria-invalid, aria-required, aria-current, and aria-busy. Key properties include aria-label, aria-labelledby, aria-describedby, aria-controls, aria-owns, aria-haspopup, aria-level, aria-orientation, aria-placeholder, aria-roledescription, and aria-keyshortcuts.
standards.intent: Provides assistive technologies with real-time information about the state and characteristics of interactive elements, enabling users to understand the current status and available interactions.
techniques: Update aria-expanded when collapsible sections are opened or closed
Set aria-selected on the active tab, option, or grid cell
Toggle aria-checked on custom checkboxes and switches
Use aria-pressed for toggle buttons
Set aria-disabled=true instead of the disabled attribute on custom controls
Use aria-hidden=true to hide decorative or redundant content from assistive tech
Set aria-invalid=true when form validation fails
Use aria-current=page for the current page in navigation
Set aria-haspopup to indicate elements that trigger popups
Use aria-controls to relate a control to the content it manages
failures: aria-expanded not updated when accordion section opens
aria-selected not set on active tab
Missing aria-checked on custom checkbox
aria-hidden=true on visible interactive content
Using aria-disabled without actual prevention of interaction
aria-invalid not set when validation fails
Stale ARIA states not updated after user interaction
aria-haspopup with incorrect value for popup type
testing_methods: Interact with components and verify ARIA states update in accessibility tree
Test screen reader announcements for state changes
Verify aria-expanded toggles correctly on disclosure widgets
Check aria-selected reflects current selection state
Validate aria-hidden does not hide interactive content
Verify aria-invalid matches validation state
Automated scan for required ARIA states per role

standards.name: ARIA Keyboard Interaction Patterns
standards.description: ARIA authoring practices define expected keyboard interaction patterns for each widget role. Users of assistive technologies expect consistent keyboard behavior based on the announced role. Key patterns include: tabs (arrow keys between tabs, Tab into panel), menus (arrow keys between items, Enter to activate, Escape to close), dialogs (Tab cycles within, Escape closes), trees (arrow keys navigate, Enter activates), comboboxes (arrow keys in listbox, Enter selects, Escape closes), and grids (arrow keys between cells).
standards.intent: Ensures that custom interactive components respond to keyboard input in the way users expect based on the component's role, creating a consistent and predictable experience for keyboard and assistive technology users.
techniques: Tabs: Left/Right arrows move between tabs, Tab moves into active panel, Home/End for first/last tab
Menus: Down arrow opens menu, Up/Down navigate items, Enter/Space activates, Escape closes, Right arrow opens submenu
Dialogs: Tab cycles focus within dialog, Escape closes, initial focus on first focusable or dialog itself
Trees: Up/Down arrows navigate siblings, Right expands/enters children, Left collapses/exits to parent
Combobox: Down arrow opens listbox, Up/Down navigate options, Enter selects, Escape closes listbox
Grid: Arrow keys navigate cells, Enter enters edit mode, Escape exits edit mode
Toolbar: Left/Right arrows move between tools, Tab moves focus in and out
Use roving tabindex (tabindex=0 on active, tabindex=-1 on inactive) for composite widgets
Implement aria-activedescendant as alternative to roving tabindex
failures: Tab component navigable only by Tab key instead of arrow keys
Menu that does not close on Escape
Dialog that does not trap focus within
Tree view without arrow key navigation
Combobox that does not filter or navigate with arrow keys
Custom listbox without Home/End key support
Missing roving tabindex on composite widget children
Grid cells not navigable by arrow keys
testing_methods: Test each custom widget against ARIA Authoring Practices keyboard patterns
Verify arrow key navigation in composite widgets
Test Escape key behavior in menus, dialogs, and popovers
Verify Enter/Space activation on interactive elements
Check roving tabindex or aria-activedescendant implementation
Test Home/End key support where applicable
Verify Tab key behavior for entering and leaving composite widgets
Test with screen reader to verify keyboard shortcuts are announced
